video_title: AI’s Speed Crash Is Creating the Next Opportunity
gist: Jordi Visser argues the recent AI/semiconductor selloff was a technical 'speed crash' and sentiment cleanse within an intact structural bull market driven by exponential token demand outrunning linear (human-built) memory and power supply, not a bubble popping; he pivots to argue consumer AI agents are the next major token-demand wave and that Scott Bessent's stablecoin/tokenization speech marks a comparable inflection point for crypto.

insights.claim: The AI 'midcycle slowdown' narrative Jordi Visser had been flagging is now ending as a market surprise because positioning has already adjusted and bears are only now voicing arguments that had already been priced in.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If the slowdown thesis is now consensus rather than a surprise, the informational edge in staying bearish has faded and the setup favors re-engaging with AI-exposed names on weakness.

insights.claim: Samsung's rolling 10-day rate of change fell roughly 21%, the largest drawdown outside the COVID panic (25-26%) and comparable only to the period just after the Lehman Brothers collapse, despite occurring inside what Visser calls a structural bull market.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A crash-magnitude drawdown inside an intact uptrend is the signature of a 'speed crash' (fast deleveraging) rather than a trend reversal, arguing for buying rather than fading the move.
insights.quote: This was 21. The only other time that it was this big on a 10-day basis was just after Lehman Brothers.

insights.claim: Retail investors using 2x-leveraged exposure to AI/semiconductor names gave back about 62% of gains during the pullback even though the underlying stocks (Samsung, SK Hynix) only fell roughly 23-38%, indicating retail leverage has already been flushed out.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Capitulation-level deleveraging in the most levered cohort (retail) typically marks a point closer to a bottom than a top, supporting adding exposure rather than reducing it.
insights.quote: The retail side using the two times levered gave back 62%.

insights.claim: Systematic long-short (quantitative) hedge fund managers lost 2.1% in a single week and 3.1% over a five-day stretch, their worst run since December 2023, but remain up on the year overall.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The drawdown among quant funds looks like a technical/positioning unwind rather than a fundamental panic, since these managers are still net positive for the year.

insights.claim: Jeff DeGraaf's work showed nearly 87% of S&P 500 semiconductor stocks were oversold on a 14-day basis during the pullback, a breadth extreme that has historically marked good bottoms in all but two prior instances.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Extreme oversold breadth readings are a tactical signal worth tracking for semiconductor entry points, even though the indicator isn't infallible.
insights.quote: Nearly 87% of S&P 500 semiconductor issues registered and oversold on a 14-day basis.

insights.claim: Within Visser's own 100-name AI thematic portfolio, the percentage of names above their 20-day moving average collapsed from 73% to 21% before rebounding to 31%, while the percentage above the 200-day moving average (his structural bull-market gauge) stayed elevated at 86%, arguing the primary uptrend is intact despite the short-term damage.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Short-term breadth washed out to oversold levels while the longer-term structural breadth measure held up, which Visser treats as confirmation the correction is a pause, not a trend break.

insights.claim: Analysts believe Samsung's 2026 operating profit could exceed $217 billion, more than the company's cumulative operating profit over the prior 40 years combined, yet the stock trades at roughly a 4x forward P/E, implying the memory earnings power is not being priced in.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A stock pricing in a near-term cyclical peak while consensus earnings estimates imply record profitability is a mispricing an investor could act on if they believe elevated memory prices persist.
insights.quote: Samsung's operating profit in 2026 could be over $217 billion. The company could generate more operating profit this year than its cumulative operating profit over the past 40 years.

insights.claim: Micron's shortage guidance for memory chips has extended from 'persisting until 2030' (stated in March) to 'persisting beyond 2030' as of the most recent update, while memory capacity is only growing 20-30% a year against demand that is doubling.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Micron management)
insights.so_what: A widening, not narrowing, supply-demand gap in memory supports a multi-year higher-for-longer pricing regime rather than a near-term cyclical peak, favoring continued exposure to memory names.

insights.claim: The current AI-driven memory cycle is fundamentally different from prior hardware cycles like the fracking/shale oil boom because oil supply was parabolic while human demand stayed linear, whereas today memory supply is capacity-constrained and linear (human-built) while AI token demand is exponential and driven by computers, not people.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If demand truly scales exponentially against linear-capped supply, standard commodity-cycle mean-reversion playbooks (like the fracking bust) don't apply, arguing against fading memory/AI hardware names on cyclicality fears.
insights.quote: The only thing that's just like fracking is the amount of people that are saying it's just like fracking.

insights.claim: Visser's core positioning framework since the start of the year has been to short 'abundance' (anything built on code that computers can replicate) and go long 'scarcity' (anything physically constrained by linear human production capacity, such as memory and power infrastructure).
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: This framework gives a repeatable screen for portfolio construction: favor physically-constrained AI infrastructure inputs over easily-replicated software/code-based businesses.
insights.quote: you want to be short abundance. Anything built on code that can be replicated by a computer. And you want to be long scarcity. Anything that the spenders are spending on that is physically constrained by linear humans.

insights.claim: Semi Analysis's Dylan Patel reported his ~90-person firm's annualized AI token spend rose from under $100,000 in November to about $4 million by January, and stands at roughly $11 million annualized today, with peak-week spend equivalent to $14 million annualized.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel (cited by Jordi Visser)
insights.so_what: A single sophisticated tech-savvy firm scaling token spend over 100x in eight months is direct evidence that 'serious user' AI adoption and monetization are accelerating well past what aggregate index-level data suggests, supporting the token-demand growth thesis.
insights.quote: By January, it was already $4 million annualized. It stands at a roughly 11 million annualized today and the peak week was equivalent to 14 million. That's for a 90 person firm.

insights.claim: Supply-side discipline from players like TSMC (unwilling to overbuild) and physical bottlenecks in power generation (e.g., gas turbines) are the constraints preventing the AI infrastructure buildout from becoming a bubble, even as demand keeps rising.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Because supply can't scale on time to meet demand, elevated pricing for AI infrastructure inputs is likely to persist rather than collapse from a supply glut.

insights.claim: Apple raised handset prices roughly 20% due to rising memory costs and is expected to sell fewer devices as a result, yet the stock still rallied back to all-time highs afterward, suggesting the market is pricing in consumer-AI/agent optionality (e.g., Siri AI features) rather than punishing the near-term margin hit.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Apple's resilience despite a clear negative catalyst (price hikes, fewer units) signals the market is willing to look through near-term memory-cost pressure toward a longer-term consumer-AI re-rating story.

themes.theme: Token Demand & the AI Macro Nexus

insights.claim: Visser's 'AI Macro Nexus' framework holds that AI is no longer just a technology sector story but has become the dominant macro force of the cycle, simultaneously shaping productivity, labor, inflation, capital spending, energy demand, digital assets, monetary policy, and market structure.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Investors analyzing inflation, labor, or Fed policy in isolation from AI's effects risk missing the dominant driver behind those data series going forward.

insights.claim: The recent decline in the AI token-expenditure index reflects compositional/seasonal factors — the end of the school year, enterprise staff vacations and remote/reduced Friday work, and a post-FOMO 'bill shock' pullback in exploratory spending — rather than a genuine slowdown in usage among AI-native, serious users.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reading the token index decline as demand destruction would be a misdiagnosis; investors should instead track adoption among heavy/professional users (like Semi Analysis) rather than the aggregate index.

insights.claim: Visser argues the token expenditure index has been misused as a proxy for AI demand health and expects it to become increasingly irrelevant ('go to zero') as a market-moving metric over time.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Traders reacting to token-index moves as a demand signal may be trading a flawed indicator; the metric's declining relevance argues for discounting headlines built around it.

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs has reportedly estimated that AI agents could drive token consumption up roughly 24x, to around 120 quadrillion tokens per month, positioning consumer AI agents as a far larger token-demand wave than the coding-agent boom that followed Claude Opus 4.5.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Goldman Sachs)
insights.so_what: If even a fraction of this token-demand growth materializes, it implies substantially more upside for memory, compute, and power infrastructure spend than current AI-infrastructure valuations may reflect.
insights.quote: Goldman has reportedly estimated that AI agents could drive token consumption up roughly 24 times to around 120 quadrillion per month.

insights.claim: Consumer AI adoption has been slow not because the underlying models are weak but because the product/interface layer is still too immature — for agents to gain habitual use they need to disappear into daily life (like search, maps, or a camera) rather than requiring users to actively open an app.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Andrew Bosworth, Meta CTO)
insights.so_what: The bottleneck for the next token-demand wave is a solvable UX/product problem, not a model-capability ceiling, meaning the catalyst for a consumer-agent token explosion could arrive faster than fundamentals-only analysis would suggest.

insights.claim: Beyond consumer software agents, full self-driving robotaxis and humanoid robots represent a further, larger step-function increase in AI compute demand because they rely on vision-language-model (VLM) video compute rather than text tokens, and this wave has barely begun.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Investors modeling AI compute demand purely on LLM/chat token growth are likely underestimating total future compute needs once autonomous vehicles and robotics scale.

insights.claim: Dylan Patel argues enterprises facing AI budget overruns should respond by cutting other SaaS/legacy software spend rather than cutting AI spend, and that the optimal model choice depends on workload type: cheaper models for fixed/repeatable workflows, but the smartest (most expensive) model can be cheaper overall for assistant-style work because it needs fewer iterations and less human correction time.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel (cited by Jordi Visser)
insights.so_what: This reframes 'AI is too expensive' complaints as a workload-allocation problem, not a cost-of-AI problem, which argues against assuming rising AI spend will crowd out enterprise profitability broadly.

insights.claim: Box CEO Aaron Levie's framework holds that closed/frontier models will keep dominating early-stage, novel-use-case discovery while open-source models win once use cases mature into predictable production workflows — but current data shows this shift is not yet happening at scale, since enterprise open-source model spend actually fell to 11% from 19%.
insights.speaker: Aaron Levie (cited by Jordi Visser)
insights.so_what: The popular 'open source is eating closed models' narrative is directionally right long-term but currently wrong in the spending data, meaning closed-model providers' enterprise revenue is not yet under near-term pressure from open source.

insights.claim: AI-native hyper-growth companies (cited example: Jesse Zhang's firm) already run about 90% of their AI workloads on open-source models instead of OpenAI or Anthropic, showing a divergence between AI-native cost optimization and slower-moving traditional enterprise adoption of closed models.
insights.speaker: Jesse Zhang (cited by Jordi Visser)
insights.so_what: This gap between AI-native and traditional-enterprise model choice is a leading indicator investors can track for when open-source substitution risk starts to bite closed-model providers' enterprise revenue.
insights.quote: we run 90% of our workloads on open-source models instead of open AI or anthropic.

insights.claim: Despite narratives of AI compute oversupply (partly fueled by reports Meta might sell some capacity), Microsoft, Google, and Amazon all indicated in their most recent earnings that demand exceeds available capacity — Microsoft stated customer demand 'continues to exceed capacity,' Google is allocating compute under a strict ROIC framework, and AWS's Trainium chips are largely sold out with much of Trainium 3 already reserved.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Hard evidence of persistent excess demand from the three largest hyperscalers directly undercuts the 'AI capacity glut' bear case that partly drove the recent selloff.
insights.quote: strong customer demand continues to exceed capacity

insights.claim: Disclosed AI infrastructure commitment backlogs stand at roughly $627 billion for Microsoft, $462 billion for Google, $225 billion for Amazon, and $638 billion for Oracle, reflecting still-enormous forward capex intentions despite the recent AI-stock pullback.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: These backlogs represent multi-year visible revenue/spend commitments for AI infrastructure suppliers (chips, memory, power), supporting continued demand for scarcity-constrained inputs.

insights.claim: Semi Analysis has raised its total AI capex buildout forecast to $11.1 trillion, well above Goldman Sachs' $7.6 trillion estimate, with Visser noting the rate of growth (the 'second derivative') will slow each year even as the absolute spending level stays enormous.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Semi Analysis)
insights.so_what: Decelerating growth rates should not be mistaken for declining spend in absolute terms — the scale of forecasted capex still supports the scarcity/infrastructure trade even as year-over-year growth normalizes.

themes.theme: Crypto, Stablecoins & the New Financial System

insights.claim: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's recent speech at the New York Economic Club, stating that digital assets, stablecoins, and tokenization will 'help shape the future of money,' marks a policy inflection point Visser compares to the early, dismissed recognition of the AI trend — prompting him to launch a new crypto-focused YouTube format.
insights.speaker: Scott Bessent (quoted by Jordi Visser)
insights.so_what: A sitting Treasury Secretary explicitly endorsing stablecoins and tokenization as core to future monetary policy is a signal investors with 'no view' on crypto can no longer ignore, according to Visser.
insights.quote: Digital assets, stable coins, tokenization, new payment system will help to shape the future of money.

insights.claim: Visser argues the Bessent speech and Mohamed El-Erian's commentary on it reframe crypto from a speculative sideshow into a tool of U.S. financial statecraft, with the administration favoring a 'regulate, don't fight' approach to dollar-based stablecoins as a way to extend dollar dominance rather than a threat to it.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If U.S. policy is actively building dollar-backed stablecoin infrastructure as strategic policy, stablecoin-adjacent assets and rails become a geopolitically-favored growth area rather than a regulatory target.
insights.quote: crypto is being reframed from a speculative sideshow into a tool of US financial statecraft.

insights.claim: Visser draws a direct parallel between Marc Andreessen's January 2014 essay 'Why Bitcoin Matters' and today's Bessent speech, arguing Bitcoin is roughly 12 years into an adoption curve analogous to the internet, which wasn't broadly 'accepted' as real until the iPhone arrived roughly a decade after 1993.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If Bitcoin follows a technology-adoption timeline similar to PCs or the internet, being 12 years in could still be relatively early in the value-accrual curve rather than late.

insights.claim: Visser has built a 40-name crypto index spanning 8 sector verticals (34 private/token assets and 6 public equities such as Circle), overlaid against Bitcoin, and describes Bitcoin as 'the S&P 500 of the future of tokenization' — a benchmark proxy for the broader tokenized-asset economy.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Investors seeking beta exposure to the tokenization theme without picking individual private/token names can use Bitcoin as the index-level proxy per this framework.
insights.quote: bitcoin is the S&P 500 of the future of tokenization

insights.claim: Visser frames agentic AI commerce — AI agents autonomously consuming compute, managing subscriptions, and settling invoices — as the 'accelerant' that will force adoption of programmable payment rails, making stablecoins and tokenization the operating layer of the emerging 'agent economy.'
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: This directly links the AI and crypto investment theses: growth in autonomous AI agent transactions should be a structural demand driver for stablecoin/payment infrastructure, not just a speculative crypto narrative.

insights.claim: Bitcoin remains technically in a bear market; Visser is watching the 50-day moving average as the immediate line in the sand (with the 200-day as the next hurdle), and notes that when Michael Saylor sold roughly 3,500 Bitcoin — a scenario many feared would crash the price — Bitcoin instead closed higher that day.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A positive price reaction to a large insider sale is a resilience signal Visser treats similarly to the Samsung 'good news that still sold off' example — evidence that bad news is being absorbed rather than triggering further downside.

insights.claim: Dogecoin, which Visser uses as a gauge of retail speculative 'energy' in crypto, remains well below both its 20-day and 200-day moving averages, indicating retail speculative appetite has not yet returned to the crypto market even as Bitcoin stabilizes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The absence of retail speculative froth (via Dogecoin) suggests crypto sentiment is still washed out rather than frothy, arguing there is room to run before a sentiment-driven top.

insights.claim: Visser assigns close to 50% probability that the Fed hikes rates in July — the only month he thinks a hike is realistic this cycle, to establish new leadership's credibility — and believes any hike would be a one-and-done 25bp move likely followed by long-term rates falling on restored Fed credibility, which he sees as a tailwind for Bitcoin if the Fed instead does nothing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A one-and-done July hike followed by falling long-term rates, or no hike at all, are both scenarios Visser frames as ultimately supportive for Bitcoin and risk assets, giving investors a rates-based catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: I think there'll be some sort of wink wink nod nod even though he said he's not going to do any forward guidance

insights.claim: Visser significantly reduced his Micron position in mid-May, ahead of the eventual price top, reallocating some proceeds into silver and Bitcoin (which he says 'haven't worked out as well'), and now plans to selectively re-add exposure across his concentrated 10-name basket given the technical reset.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Visser's own trade illustrates a tactical trim-into-strength, rotate-on-weakness approach investors can study, though it also shows his silver/Bitcoin reallocation underperformed relative to expectations.

insights.claim: Nvidia peaked on May 14th, roughly three weeks before the broader AI-infrastructure trade topped and more than a month before SMH and memory names peaked, and is now closing above its 20-day moving average for the first time since June 2nd, holding its 200-day, with a MACD buy signal at its lowest valuation in a decade.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Nvidia's tendency to lead turns in the broader AI-infrastructure trade makes it a useful leading indicator, and its current technical buy signals argue for re-entry per Visser's concentrated basket.

insights.claim: Visser still holds Palantir (bought around $130 in Q1, near its February earnings) despite acknowledging the company is 'talking its own book' on data sovereignty (arguing firms should own their own data before Anthropic or OpenAI 'steal it'), because he finds the broader thesis compelling enough to keep it in his 100-name portfolio.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Even a self-interested corporate narrative can reflect a real structural trend (enterprise data sovereignty versus AI vendors), which Visser treats as reason enough to maintain a position despite the stock consolidating for months.
